title: When You Realize Your Parents Spent Your College Fund
dek: The betrayal does not live in the bank account. It lives in the jaw, the psoas, the sudden inability to swallow. When the people who were supposed to protect your future consume it instead, the body registers the theft as a physical blow. Here is how we begin to metabolize the unthinkable.
bodyMarkdown:
You are sitting at the kitchen table, or perhaps standing in the hallway with the mail in your hand, when the realization finally lands. The money is gone. The fund that was supposed to be your safety net, your launchpad, the tangible proof that your future was considered and protected - it has been consumed. By the very people who promised to hold it for you. The betrayal does not register first in your mind. It registers in the body. Specifically, it hits the diaphragm. You stop breathing. The large, dome-shaped muscle at the base of your lungs seizes, freezing in place as if trying to halt time itself. Your jaw clenches, the masseter muscles pulling tight, locking your teeth together. This is not a metaphor. This is biology.


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.236Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

The theft of your future is a physical event. When you realize your parents spent your college fund, the shock wave travels down the vagus nerve, signaling to your viscera that the world is fundamentally unsafe. The people who were supposed to be your primary source of security have become the source of your profound vulnerability. Your psoas muscles - the deep core muscles that connect your spine to your legs, the ones responsible for the fight-or-flight response - contract violently. They are preparing you to run, or to kick, but there is nowhere to run and no one you can safely strike. So the tension stays locked in the tissue. You carry the betrayal in your hips, in the tightness of your lower back, in the sudden, sharp pain that flares up when you try to stand tall.

We are taught to intellectualize this kind of betrayal. We are told to look at the bank statements, to calculate the loss, to figure out a new plan. We are told to be pragmatic. But the body does not care about pragmatism. The body only knows that a fundamental contract has been broken. Gabor Maté writes extensively about how our early environments shape our physiological responses to stress. He notes that when our primary caregivers fail us, the resulting trauma is not just psychological; it is embedded in our neurobiology. The betrayal becomes a part of our cellular memory. When the people who are supposed to protect us instead exploit us, the resulting confusion and rage are toxic to the nervous system. The body is forced to choose between attachment and authenticity, and in the face of such a profound betrayal, authenticity often means feeling a rage so immense it threatens to consume us.

This rage is not a moral failure. It is a biological imperative. It is the body's way of saying, *I matter. My future matters. What was done to me is unacceptable.* But we are so rarely allowed to feel this rage. Women, especially, are taught that our fury is ugly, dangerous, and unspiritual. We are told to forgive, to move on, to understand that our parents were doing the best they could. But the body knows the truth. The body knows that 'doing their best' is a cognitive rationalization that does nothing to soothe the inflamed nervous system. The body requires acknowledgment. It requires the rage to be felt, to be moved, to be metabolized.

Consider the physical sensation of this specific anger. It is not a hot, explosive fire. It is a cold, heavy stone in the pit of the stomach. It is the feeling of the ground dropping out from under you. It is the sudden, terrifying realization that you are entirely alone. This is where the herbs come in. Not as a cure, but as an ally. When the nervous system is locked in this kind of freeze response, we need plants that can gently coax the body back into a state of flow. [FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a profound ally in this work. Skullcap (*Scutellaria lateriflora*) is a nervine trophorestorative. It does not just sedate; it rebuilds the frayed edges of the nervous system. It is the herbal equivalent of a heavy blanket and a steady hand on your shoulder. When you drink it, you can feel the tension in the trapezius muscles begin to soften, just a fraction of an inch.

The betrayal of financial theft by a parent is uniquely devastating because it is a theft of agency. Money, in our society, is a proxy for freedom, for choice, for the ability to move through the world on your own terms. When your parents spend your college fund, they are not just taking dollars; they are taking your choices. They are narrowing your horizon. This narrowing is felt in the chest. The intercostal muscles, the small muscles between your ribs, tighten, restricting your breath. You begin to breathe shallowly, sipping air into the top of your lungs, never fully expanding. This shallow breathing keeps you in a state of low-grade panic, a constant vigilance.

Resmaa Menakem, in his groundbreaking work on somatic abolitionism, teaches us that trauma is not just an event that happened in the past; it is a living, breathing reality in the body right now. He speaks of the 'soul nerve' - the vagus nerve - and how it holds the physical imprint of our experiences. When we experience a profound betrayal, the soul nerve constricts. Menakem emphasizes that we cannot simply think our way out of this constriction. We must feel our way out. We must engage the body in the process of healing. This means paying attention to the physical sensations of the betrayal, without trying to fix them or push them away. It means noticing the tightness in the throat, the fluttering in the stomach, the numbness in the hands, and saying to the body, *I feel you. I am here with you.*

This is the work of somatic tracking. It is the slow, deliberate process of bringing your awareness to the physical sensations of your anger and your grief. When you realize the money is gone, you might feel a sudden urge to scream, but the scream gets trapped in your throat. The scalene muscles in your neck become rigid. Your vocal cords tighten. You swallow the scream, because to let it out would be to acknowledge the full magnitude of the loss. But the swallowed scream does not disappear. It turns into a chronic ache, a persistent tension headache, a feeling of being constantly on edge.

To begin to move this trapped energy, we must create a container of safety. The body will not release its grip if it does not feel safe. This is where the daily, rhythmic practices of herbalism and somatic awareness become essential. You might begin by simply placing a hand on your chest and a hand on your belly, and noticing the movement of your breath. You do not need to change the breath; just notice it. Notice how the body responds to the warmth of your hands. Notice if there is any softening in the jaw, any dropping of the shoulders.

We can also call on the adaptogens, the plants that help the body build resilience in the face of chronic stress. [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a powerful tool here. Ashwagandha (*Withania somnifera*) is a root that grounds us. It speaks to the adrenal glands, the small, triangular organs sitting on top of the kidneys that have been pumping out cortisol and adrenaline since the moment you realized the money was gone. Ashwagandha says to the adrenals, *You can rest now. The immediate threat has passed.* It helps to lower the baseline level of hyperarousal, allowing the body to begin the slow work of repair.

The anger you feel toward your parents is not a sign that you are ungrateful or unforgiving. It is a sign that your body is functioning exactly as it should. Anger is the boundary-keeper. It is the emotion that arises when a boundary has been violated. And what greater violation is there than the theft of your future by the people who were supposed to protect it? Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal work on trauma, reminds us that the body keeps the score. The cognitive brain can be tricked, manipulated, and gaslit. Your parents might tell you that they needed the money, that they will pay it back, that you are overreacting. But the body does not lie. The body knows that a profound injustice has occurred. The body holds the truth of the betrayal in the tension of the fascia, in the chronic inflammation, in the disrupted sleep patterns.

When we try to bypass this anger, when we try to jump straight to forgiveness or understanding, we do violence to ourselves. We abandon the body in its moment of greatest need. We must learn to sit with the fury. We must learn to let it move through us, not as a destructive force, but as a clarifying fire. This means finding safe ways to express the physical energy of the anger. It might mean screaming into a pillow, or pushing against a wall with all your strength, feeling the engagement of the latissimus dorsi and the triceps. It might mean shaking your hands and feet, literally shaking the excess adrenaline out of your nervous system.

And as we do this work, we must nourish the body. The sheer metabolic cost of carrying this kind of betrayal is immense. The body is burning through its reserves of minerals and vitamins just to keep you upright. This is why I rely so heavily on nourishing herbal infusions. [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) is my foundation. Nettle (*Urtica dioica*) is a powerhouse of nutrition. It is rich in calcium, magnesium, iron, and trace minerals. When you steep a large handful of nettle leaf in hot water overnight and drink the dark, earthy infusion the next day, you are literally pouring minerals back into your depleted tissues. You are telling the body, *I am feeding you. I am taking care of you, even when others did not.*

The process of metabolizing this betrayal is not linear. There will be days when you feel you have moved past it, and then a sudden memory, a passing comment, or a glance at a bank statement will send you right back into the freeze response. The diaphragm will seize again. The jaw will clench. This is not a failure. This is the nature of trauma. The body processes the pain in layers, taking only what it can handle at any given moment. Your job is not to rush the process. Your job is to stay present with the body, to offer it the support and the witnessing it needs.

We must also address the profound grief that accompanies this anger. The anger is the protective shell; the grief is the soft, vulnerable underbelly. You are not just grieving the loss of the money. You are grieving the loss of the parents you thought you had. You are grieving the illusion of safety. This grief is heavy. It settles in the lungs, making it hard to take a deep breath. It settles in the heart space, creating a physical ache in the center of the chest. In Traditional Chinese Medicine, the lungs are the organ associated with grief. When we are holding onto unresolved sorrow, the lung meridian becomes blocked. We might experience chronic coughs, asthma, or a persistent feeling of tightness in the chest.

To support the lungs, we must allow the grief to move. This means allowing the tears to come, not just the quiet, polite tears, but the deep, wracking sobs that shake the entire body. It means allowing the diaphragm to spasm, allowing the intercostal muscles to stretch and release. It means making sound. The body needs to vocalize the pain in order to release it. And as we do this, we can use aromatic herbs to help open the airways and move the stagnant energy. The scent of pine, of eucalyptus, of thyme - these are the allies of the lungs. They remind the body how to breathe deeply again.

The realization that your parents spent your college fund is a rupture in the fabric of your reality. It forces you to reevaluate everything you thought you knew about your family, your safety, and your future. But it is also an opportunity. It is an opportunity to reclaim your agency, to build a foundation of safety within your own body, rather than relying on the fragile promises of others. It is an opportunity to become your own fierce protector.

This reclamation begins in the body. It begins with the simple, radical act of noticing your physical sensations without judgment. It begins with the willingness to feel the anger, the grief, the terror, and the profound disappointment. It begins with the understanding that your body is not broken, but is responding perfectly to a heartbreaking situation. You are not a victim of your biology; you are a participant in your own healing.

As you move through this process, remember that you are not alone. The lineage of women who have had to build their own safety, who have had to metabolize the betrayals of those who were supposed to protect them, is long and deep. You are tapping into a wellspring of ancestral resilience. Your body knows how to do this. The intelligence that beats your heart and heals your wounds knows how to metabolize this pain. Your task is simply to get out of the way, to stop suppressing the truth, and to let the body do its work.

## The Anatomy of Betrayal

Let us look closer at the specific anatomy of this betrayal. When the realization hits, the sympathetic nervous system kicks into high gear. The amygdala, the brain's threat-detection center, sounds the alarm. The hypothalamus signals the pituitary gland, which in turn signals the adrenal glands to release a flood of cortisol and adrenaline. Your heart rate increases. Your blood pressure spikes. Blood is shunted away from your digestive organs and toward your extremities, preparing you to fight or flee.

But there is no physical enemy to fight, and nowhere to run. The threat is abstract, yet the physical response is entirely concrete. This creates a profound dissonance in the body. The energy of the fight-or-flight response has nowhere to go, so it turns inward. It becomes the chronic tension in your neck and shoulders. It becomes the irritable bowel syndrome, the acid reflux, the inability to digest your food properly. The body is literally unable to stomach the betrayal.

This is why cognitive therapies alone are often insufficient for this kind of trauma. You can talk about the betrayal for years, you can analyze your parents' motives, you can understand the socioeconomic factors that led to their decision, but until you address the physiological reality of the trauma, the body will remain locked in a state of hyperarousal. The body needs somatic interventions. It needs movement, touch, breath, and the support of plant medicines that speak directly to the nervous system.

## The Somatic Practice of Reclaiming Ground

Here is a short, embodied practice to help you begin to move the trapped energy of this betrayal. Do this practice when you are feeling overwhelmed by the anger or the grief, when the jaw is tight and the breath is shallow.

Find a quiet place where you will not be disturbed. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent. Do not lock your knees. Feel the contact between the soles of your feet and the floor. Notice the texture of the floor, the temperature, the solidity of it. This is your ground. It is here, it is holding you, and it will not be taken away.

Bring your awareness to your psoas muscles, deep in your core. Imagine a line of energy connecting your psoas down through your legs and into the earth. Take a slow, deep breath in, allowing the belly to expand. As you exhale, make a low, resonant sound - a hum or a groan. Let the sound vibrate in your chest and your belly. Do not worry about sounding pretty. Let the sound be ugly, guttural, real.

As you continue to breathe and sound, begin to gently shake your body. Start with your hands, shaking them as if you are trying to flick water off your fingers. Let the shaking move up your arms, into your shoulders, your torso, your hips, your legs. Shake vigorously. Let the jaw hang loose. Let the head bob. You are literally shaking the excess adrenaline out of your nervous system. You are telling the body, *The threat is over. We can discharge this energy now.*

Continue shaking for two to three minutes, or until you feel a shift in your energy. Then, slowly come to a stop. Stand still and notice the sensations in your body. You might feel tingling, warmth, or a sense of lightness. You might feel a sudden wave of exhaustion. Whatever you feel, simply notice it. Place one hand on your heart and one hand on your belly, and take three slow, deep breaths. Thank your body for its hard work.

## The Long Arc of Healing

Healing from this kind of betrayal is not a destination; it is a continuous process of returning to the body, returning to the truth, returning to your own inherent worth. There will be setbacks. There will be days when the anger feels as fresh and as raw as the day you first found out. When those days come, do not judge yourself. Simply return to the practices. Brew the skullcap tea. Take the ashwagandha. Do the shaking practice. Feel the ground beneath your feet.

You are building a new foundation, one that is not dependent on the flawed promises of others. You are learning to mother yourself, to protect yourself, to fiercely advocate for your own well-being. This is the ultimate alchemy. You are taking the raw, toxic material of betrayal and transmuting it into the gold of somatic sovereignty. The money may be gone, but your body, your breath, your life force - these belong entirely to you. And no one can ever spend them but you.

## The Fascia Holds the Memory

We must talk about the fascia. The connective tissue that wraps every muscle, every organ, every nerve in your body. Fascia is not just inert wrapping paper; it is a highly innervated, dynamic communication system. When you experience a shock - like the sudden realization that your financial foundation has been pulled out from under you - the fascia contracts. It tightens to create a physical armor, bracing you against the blow. This bracing pattern becomes locked in the tissue. You might notice that your posture has changed since you found out. Perhaps your shoulders are permanently hitched up toward your ears, the levator scapulae muscles chronically shortened. Perhaps your chest is caved in, protecting the vulnerable heart space. This is the fascia holding the memory of the betrayal.

To release this fascial holding, we cannot use force. If you try to aggressively stretch or massage the tissue, the body will perceive it as another attack and will tighten further. We must use patience, warmth, and sustained, gentle pressure. This is where somatic unwinding comes in. It is the practice of allowing the body to move in slow, spontaneous ways, following the subtle pulls and unwinding patterns of the fascia. It is a deep listening to the tissue. As the fascia begins to hydrate and release, you may experience sudden waves of emotion - tears, shaking, or a sudden, sharp exhalation. This is the tissue releasing the trapped energy of the trauma.

## The Role of the Liver in Traditional Chinese Medicine

In Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), anger is the emotion associated with the Liver. The Liver is responsible for the smooth flow of Qi (energy) and blood throughout the body. When we experience profound anger, especially anger that we feel we cannot express, the Liver Qi becomes stagnant. This stagnation manifests physically as tension in the neck and shoulders, headaches, digestive issues, and menstrual irregularities. It manifests emotionally as irritability, frustration, and a feeling of being stuck.

To support the Liver and encourage the smooth flow of Qi, we must move the body. Brisk walking, dancing, or any movement that gets the blood pumping and the breath moving is essential. We can also use bitter herbs, which stimulate the Liver and encourage detoxification. Dandelion root, burdock root, and yellow dock are excellent allies here. They remind the Liver of its function, helping it to process and clear not just physical toxins, but the emotional toxicity of the betrayal.

## The Illusion of the 'Good Daughter'

Many of us were raised with the implicit contract that if we were 'good' - if we were compliant, achieving, and undemanding - we would be protected. The theft of the college fund shatters this contract. It reveals the terrifying truth that your compliance did not buy you safety. This realization often triggers a profound identity crisis. Who are you, if not the 'good daughter'? What is your worth, if the people who raised you treated your future as disposable?

This identity crisis is felt in the solar plexus, the center of our personal power and autonomy. You might feel a hollow, sinking sensation just below your sternum. This is the energetic center of your self-worth collapsing under the weight of the betrayal. Rebuilding this center requires a fierce, unapologetic reclamation of your own value. It requires you to stop seeking validation from the people who harmed you, and to start sourcing your worth from within. It requires you to become the author of your own life, rather than a character in your parents' narrative.

## The Necessity of Boundaries

You cannot heal in the same environment that made you sick. If you are still in close contact with the parents who spent your fund, and if they are continuing to gaslight you, minimize the theft, or demand your compliance, your nervous system will remain in a state of chronic threat. You must establish boundaries. A boundary is not a punishment; it is a parameter for safety. It is the physical and emotional distance required for your nervous system to down-regulate.

Setting boundaries is a somatic act. It requires you to feel the 'no' in your body. It requires you to feel the solidity of your own spine, the strength of your own legs. When you say 'no' to further exploitation, you are not just speaking words; you are taking a physical stance. You are occupying space. You are declaring, *This is where I end, and you begin. You may not cross this line.* This is terrifying work, especially if you have been conditioned to be endlessly accommodating. But it is the only way to build a life that is truly your own.

The work of healing is the work of returning to the body. It is the slow, patient process of learning to listen to the whispers of the fascia, the deep rhythms of the organs, the subtle shifts in the nervous system. When the betrayal is this profound, when the theft is this intimate, the body is the only reliable compass we have left. We must learn to trust its signals, to honor its boundaries, and to fiercely protect its right to exist, to thrive, and to feel the full spectrum of its own wild, unapologetic truth. 

## FAQ: The Body's Questions

**Q: I feel guilty for being so angry. My parents say they needed the money to survive. Am I being selfish?**
**A:** The guilt you feel is a secondary emotion, a learned response designed to keep you compliant and to protect the family system from the disruptive force of your anger. Your anger is not selfish; it is a biological boundary-keeper. It is the body's accurate response to a profound violation of trust. You can hold compassion for your parents' struggles while simultaneously honoring the absolute validity of your fury. The two are not mutually exclusive. But the body must be allowed to feel the anger first, without the dampening effect of guilt.

**Q: I can't sleep since I found out. My mind races and my body twitches. What can I do?**
**A:** Your nervous system is stuck in a sympathetic dominant state (fight-or-flight). The twitching is the body's attempt to discharge the trapped survival energy. Do not try to force sleep; this will only increase the anxiety. Instead, focus on soothing the nervous system. Drink a strong infusion of nervine herbs like skullcap or passionflower before bed. Practice the shaking exercise described above to help discharge the adrenaline. And consider using a weighted blanket, which provides deep pressure stimulation that can help calm the vagus nerve.

**Q: I feel completely numb. I know I should be angry, but I just feel empty and disconnected from my body. Is this normal?**
**A:** Yes, this is entirely normal. Numbness is the body's ultimate defense mechanism. When the pain or the threat is too overwhelming for the nervous system to process, it goes into a dorsal vagal freeze response. It shuts down to protect you from the unbearable intensity of the emotion. Do not try to force yourself to feel angry. Instead, gently coax the body out of the freeze state through slow, mindful movement, gentle touch, and grounding practices. The anger will surface when the body feels safe enough to process it.

**Q: How do I ever trust anyone again, especially with money or my future?**
**A:** Trust is not a cognitive decision; it is a somatic experience. Right now, your body's neuroception - its subconscious ability to detect safety or danger - is highly sensitized. It is perceiving danger everywhere, which is a logical response to what happened. Rebuilding trust begins with rebuilding trust in yourself. It begins with proving to your body that you will listen to its signals, that you will honor its boundaries, and that you will protect it. As you build this internal safety, your capacity to discern safe people and safe situations in the external world will gradually return.
